title: Pannobile – meeting this coalition of amazing Burgenland producers, who are making some of Austria’s most interesting wines
summary: The Pannobile group is now 30 years old. It was John Nittnaus who created the term Pannobile, from Pannonia, the
content:
The Pannobile group is now 30 years old. It was John Nittnaus who created the term Pannobile, from Pannonia, the historical name of the region, a Roman province in western Hungary, and Nobile which defines the group’s philosophy.

It’s an association of eight wineries in Burgenland, all of who seek to make interesting wines that are representative of place.

A group of us gathered at the Preisinger winery in Gols to taste through a selection of their wines, arranged in themed flights. Very few of the wines bear the regional names, though, because they don’t meet the very strict Austrian wine law hoops that you have to jump through if you want to put appellation names on the bottle.

A short film of the tasting:

This was a brilliant tasting with some really lovely wines.

SPARKLING

Judith Beck Pet Nat Bambule! 2024 Österreich
90% Pinot Blanc, direct pressed, plus 10% skin fermented Muskat Ottonel. Cloudy, bright and complex. Nice volume and some spicy detail with apple and pear fruit. Real texture and interest. 93/100

Gsellmann Pet Nat OMG Rosé 2024 Österreich
Sankt Laurent. Pink/orange in colour. Nice tart cherry fruit with some spice. Fruity and expressive with a salty, yeasty edge and real interest. Good acidity. Tangy and bright. 93/100

Pittnauer Pitt Nat Blanc 2025 Weinland
There’s some skin-fermented Sauvignon in this blend. Cloudy and grapey with some richness. Tangy and bright with a pithy edge. Fresh and nicely edgy, and clean. 91/100

WHITE. WHAT ELSE?

RennerSistas Intergalactic 2025  Weinland
This is a blend of Grüner Veltline, Chardonnay, Welschriesling, Pinot Blanc and Muskat Ottonel. Slightly cloudy, this is textural with appealing pear and white peach fruit, with a slight saltiness. Bright, with lovely texture and freshness. 94/100

Claus Preisinger Fruitloops weiß 2024Österreich
This is a blend of Traminer, Muscat and Scheurebe, all fermented on skins for 5-7 days, then aged in a mix of concrete eggs and amphora. Lovely texture with spice and structure, and interesting aromatics, with layers of tannin and some juicy refinement. 94/100

Pittnauer Perfect Day 2025 Österreich
Muskat Ottonel, Gelber Muskateller and Sauvignon Blanc, skin fermented separately before blending and bottling. Some light lees are added at bottling. Juicy, stony and fine with some spicy detail and nice grainy structure. Very fine and expressive with real finesse. 94/100

WHITE AND ORIGIN

Heinrich Edelgraben 2024 Leithaberg DAC
This is Pinot Blanc and Chardonnay, one amphora of each, from mica schist soils. Low yields of 2.5 tons/hectare from 6000 vines a hectare. Complex aromatics of taut citrus fruit and a faint hint of flinty reduction. Pure, with pear and citrus fruit, showing precision, a bit of structure, and a salty finish. Brilliant wine. 96/100

Anita and Hans Nittnaus Furmint Tannenberg 2024 Burgenland
From quartzite soils in Gols, a windy, dry, warm site. Complex, layered and mineral with lovely citrus, apple and pear fruit. Nice stony detail and a sense of harmony, slightly salty on the finish. Such finesse. 95/100

Claus Preisinger ErdeLuftGrasundReben 2022 Burgenland
Grüner Veltliner, 50 years old, limestone soils. Foot stomped, then pressed next day to a 1000 litre barrel. Slightly cloudy and really textural with nice stoniness and a mineral sreak, with lovely juiciness. Peppery and mineral with nice lemony detail. 95/100

PANNOBILE WHITE

Anita and Hans Nittnaus Pannobile 2024 Burgenland
Grüner Veltliner (80%) and Furmint. Rich, bold and textural with pear, peach and apple fruit. Some saltiness. There’s a lot of depth here with a wide dynamic range from rich and ripe to salty and alert. Lovely. 94/100

Andreas Gsellmann Pannobile 2022 Weinland
Pinot Blanc raised in used oak. Fine aromatics with pear and peach fruit, as well as apple, peach and a hint of spicy citrus character. The palate has tension and focus with bold citrus fruit, finishing fresh. 94/100

Leitner Pannobile 2020 Burgenland
Pinot Blanc planted in 1988. 80% is pressed after 10 h on skins, while 20% stays on skins for 14 days. Aged in 225 and 500 litre oak. Broadly textured and quite bold with pear and peach fruit, as well as some warmth and richness on the finish. Inviting. 92/100

MORE SKIN CONTACT

Judith Beck Welschriesling Bambule! 2022 Österreich
Judith describes this central European grape variety as much neglected. Full yellow and slightly cloudy, this is highly aromatic with notes of citrus, orange peel, smoke and spice. Lively, intense and fine. 94/100

Heirich Graue Freyheit 2024 Österreich
Pinot Gris (one third) and Pinot Blanc, two weeks maceration in amphora, remove the top of the cap, then press back to the same amphora with full lees. No sulfite additions until bottling. Full orange/red in colour, this is juicy, supple and fine with nice tannins and some orange peel and grapefruit. Great balance. 95/100

Leitner Shake me! Gemischter Satz 2023 Weinland
1949 planting, with the main part from the Pinot Family. 5 days on skins. Crystalline citrus, some toast and minerals on the nose. Textured palate with pear and peach, and very fine-grained tannins. 93/100

THE LIGHTNESS OF WINE

Rennersistas PIN-OH! NOIR 2023 Weinland
Pinot Noir, two-thirds whole bunch, 4 days on skins, then fermentation finishes in 500 litre barrels. Slightly cloudy. Vivid nose of sappy, leafy redcurrants. The palate is juicy and leafy with red cherries. Lively and expressive. 94/100

Claus Preisinger Pinot Noir 2024 Österreich
From seven vineyard sites, 7 clones. Mainly whole bunch, and a mix of amphora and 600 litre barrels. Cruncy, juicy, lively and expressive with redcurrant and raspberry fruit. Grainy and chalky with good structure under the vibrant raspberry and cherry fruit. 94/100

Pittnauer Best Friend 2025 Österreich
Blaufränkisch. This is a barrel sample and it is mousy.

RED AND ORIGIN

Heinrich Alter Berg 2019 Leithaberg DAC
Blaufränkisch from fossil limestone soils, aged in amphora and 500 litre barrels. Fresh, vital and intense with lovely black cherry and blackcurrant fruit backed up by substantial tannin. Taut, reductive and intense, this is precise and mineralic. 95/100

Anita and Hans Nittnaus Blaufränkisch 2021 Leithaberg DAC
Limestone soils. Whole berry ferment, goes to 500 litre oak with full lees for 20 months. Refined and detailed, this is grainy and fine with black cherry and blackberry fruit. Firm tannins, but they aren’t aggressive. Such purity. 96/100

Andreas Gsellmann Altenberg Blaufränkisch St. Margarethen 2024 Burgenland
Fresh, sappy and aromatic with red cherries and some leafy notes. Fruity, juicy, fine and elegant on the palate with fine-grained tanins. This combines elegance, drinkability and seriousness to good effect. 94/100

PANNOBILE RED

Judith Beck Pannobile 2023 Österreich
Zweigelt and Blaufränksich. Slightly wild and spicy on the nose with bright aromatics. In the mouth this is juicy and vivid with nice intensity and grainy spicy characters as well as some phenolics. Meaty and a bit wild. 92/100

Renner Pannobile 2021 Weinland
Blaufrankisch. Leafy and spicy with some sweet herbal hints, with a bit of development – a touch of spice and earth. Mellow. 92/100

Leitner Pannobile 2020 Burgenland
Zweigelt, Blaufränkisch and Sankt Laurent. Sweet and pure with liqueur-like fruit on the nose. Supple with nice red fruits. Elegant and nicely framed with good texture. Nice juicy spicy finish, like a very ripe Barolo. 94/100

title: Restaurants: Labombe by Trivet, London
summary: Website: https://www.labombe.co.uk/ A new star on the London restaurant scene is Labombe, the sister restaurant of Michelin-two-star Trivet, overseen by
content:
Website: https://www.labombe.co.uk/

A new star on the London restaurant scene is Labombe, the sister restaurant of Michelin-two-star Trivet, overseen by chef Jonny Lake and Isa Bal looking after the wine list. Labombe has already been awarded a Michelin star.

I visited with a wine producer, so I didn’t have the normal experience of ordering from the menu and plundering the wine list, but I wanted to plug it here because the food was exceptional, and it’s a beautiful space.

It’s part of the COMO hotel on Old Park Lane, a nice location on the fringes of Mayfair. Prices are very reasonable, and in the week there’s a set lunch for £30.

The wine list, which you can view on the restaurant website, is exceptional and amazingly diverse. Very Isa!

title: United States of Blaufränkisch – a deep dive into this amazing red grape variety (1) 25 examples from Hungary
summary: Blaufränkisch is a grape variety on the rise. It has only come to prominence in recent years, for a range
content:
Blaufränkisch is a grape variety on the rise. It has only come to prominence in recent years, for a range of reasons. One is that the Burgenland region in Austria was part of Hungary until the 1920s, and so the wines went east not west. And Hungary, one of the leading countries for this variety was under communism for a long time, with 5 year plans that weren’t great for viticulture. Then we have the fact that this is a high-acid grape and in the past it struggled in cooler vintages to taste all that nice. And when Austria got going trying to make serious red wines, they went to Bordeaux varieties and new oak and overripeness, rather than recognizing the jewel they already had. Now though, people understand how good this grape is. Not everyone, but enough people.

I’ve attended a couple of Blaufränkish events in the last few years that have looked at this variety from an international perspective.

One was in the Austrian alps: https://wineanorak.com/2022/12/14/the-blaufrankisch-summit-getting-to-grips-with-this-fabulous-variety-in-the-austrian-alps/

And one in Slovenia: https://wineanorak.com/2022/10/13/blue-frank-think-tank-2-tasting-blaufrankisch-from-around-the-world/

More recently, I wrote up a report of some of Austria’s most exciting Blaufränkisch wines: https://wineanorak.com/2025/01/08/blau-a-special-exploration-into-the-world-of-blaufrankisch/

At VieVinum in Vienna, May 2026, there was a special tasting of 103 examples (see the group picture, above, which is courtesy of photographer Anna Stoecher), and I’m writing this up in three parts. We begin with Hungary.

HUNGARY

Hungary is one of the leading producers of Blaufränkisch, where it is known as Kékfrankos. This is a selection of 25 interesting wines from across the country.

Rustica Winery Kékfrankos Siller 2024 Neszmélyi, Hungary
Pale in colour with sappy red cherries and cranberries, as well as nice spicy detail. Light and tart. 90/100

Skrabski Winery Kékfrankos 2024 Balatonfüred-Csopak, Hungary
Nice concentration to this bright, vivid wine. Juicy and tart with lovely black cherries and plums, as well as good acidity. Fresh, vivid and pure. 93/100

Zelna Füred Kékfrankos 2023 Balatonfüred-Csopak, Hungary
Warm spicy nose has some nice vribrant berry fruits. Fresh palate with nice tartness and some juiciness. An intriguing wine with some grip. 91/100

Martinus Estate Ágoston Kékfrankos Válogátos 2023 Balatonfüred-Csopak, Hungary
Some spicy oak on the nose with vibrant berry fruit. Nice compact palate with good weight and density, showing glossy black fruits. 91/100

Illés Winery Kékfrankos 2023 Badacsony, Hungary
Lighter coloured. Warm candied red fruits on the nose. Supple and easy with some vanilla and red fruit character. 88/100

Etyeki Kúria MSV Konkrét Kékfrankos 2023 Etyek-Buda, Hungary
Supple, light and expressive with lovely cherry and berry fruit, and nice structure. So well balanced. 92/100

Puklusvin Winery KáeF 2024 Sopron, Hungary
Bright and vivid with nice focus to the juicy, supple fruit. A very drinkable style. 90/100

Taschner Borház Soproni Kékfrankos 2024 Sopron, Hungary
Grunty and slightly reductive berry fruits nose. Chalky and dense on the palate with a savoury, spicy edge to the berry and cherry fruit. Nice weight. 90/100

Steigler Wine Kékfrankos Prémium Vintage 2022 Sopron, Hungary
Fruity, expressive and bright with nice juicy cherry and plum fruit. Grippy and grainy eith a savoury structure. Bright on the finish. 91/100

Eszterbauer Tanyamacska 2025 Szekszárd, Hungary    
Fresh and supple with nice grip and good structure and focus with nice acidity. There’s some substance here, and a slight tarry edge to the fruit. 92/100

Eszterbauer Teihold 2023 Szekszárd, Hungary
Lovely structure here with concentrated sweet, grainy, spicy black cherry and blackberry fruit showing nice intensity. Finishes bright and tannic. 93/100

Takler Wine Estate Szenta-hegyi Kékfrankos 2023 Szekszárd, Hungary
Concentrated and bright raspberry and cherry fruit. Supple with nice freshness, finishing pure and vivid. Lovely. 93/100

Heimann Bati Kereszt Kékfrankos 2021 Szekszárd, Hungary
Juicy and supple, and nicely bright with raspberry and red cherry fruit showing nice weight. This is juicy and expressive with lovely fruit. Finishes bright. 94/100

Vida Péter Hidaspetre Kékfrankos 2022 Szekszárd, Hungary
Juicy and energetic with nice red cherry and berry fruit. Vibrant and expressive showing lovely energy and drinkability. 93/100

Lajver Winery Lajver Kékfrankos LTD 2021 Szekszárd, Hungary
Structured and grainy with nice complexity. There’s some woody, spicy depth as well as vivid berry fruits. Grainy with nice density. 92/100

Szeleshát Pincészet Kerek Kékfrankos 2022 Szekszárd, Hungary
Nice density here with substantial spice-laden cherry and berry fruits, as well as nice grip on the finish. Classic style. 91100

Tringa Wine Estate Inferno Kékfrankos Prémium 2020 Szekszárd, Hungary
Strong spicy oak imprint. Dense and quite structured with nice fruit but the charry oak is dominant. 87/100

Tüske Pince Halmai Csaba Menek Kékfrankos 2019 Szekszárd, Hungary
Supple, bright and elegant with nice juicy cherry and berry fruit with good acidity. 91/100

Vylyan Vineyards and Winery Mandolás Kékfrankos Vintage 2023 Villány, Hungary
Nice depth here: concentrated red cherry and berry fruit. Bright and not too extracted with appealing fruit, finishing fresh. 93/100

Heumann Winery Kékfrankos Reserve 2020 Villány, Hungary
Classic style with some sophisticated oak supporting rich, bold cherry fruits. Nice weight and balance with a touch of evolution. 92/100

Stier Winery Kékfrankos 2022 Villány, Hungary
Supple and spicy with nice energy and grip, showing lovely berry and cherry fruits. Bright, finishing dry and tannic. 91/100

Stier Winery Kékfrankos 2020 Villány, Hungary
Warm and sweetly fruited with some stewed strawberry notes. Nice savoury grip on the finish with some warm spiciness. 90/100

Bock Winery Kékfrankos Selection 2023 Villány, Hungary
Pretty and attractive  with sweet strawberry and cherry fruit as well as some herbal character. Very appealing in a fruity style. 92/100

Riczu Tamás Winery Villányi Kékfrankos  2022 Villány, Hungary
This is spicy and sweetly fruited with nice plush cherry and plum fruit, finishing fresh. 90/100

Jackfall Kft. Kékfrankos 2018 Villány, Hungary
This is evolving nicely with supple, well-balanced cherry and raspberry fruit, finishing spicy. 91/100

title: Vergelegen: the first release of the top wines under new winemaker Luke O’Cuinneagain
summary: I visited Vergelegen a while ago, seeing how this celebrated wine estate has emerged from a period of underperformance under
content:
I visited Vergelegen a while ago, seeing how this celebrated wine estate has emerged from a period of underperformance under the leadership of new winemaker Luke O’Cuinneagain (pictured above).

In short, Vergelegen was a huge presence on the South African wine scene back in the early 2000s, but in recent years had not delivered wines of the same quality, while other players had emerged operating at a high level. Vergelegen wasn’t where it should be, hence the appointment of Luke.

Things take time in wine, and so for the first couple of years of his tenure, all he could show was the wines from the previous regime, which weren’t very good, and the cheaper fast turnover wines. This isn’t an ideal situation, because you don’t want to be bad-mouthing wines you still have for sale in your cellar, but at the same time, it’s hard to stand behind someone else’s work.

Now we have the first releases of the top wines that were fully under his watch, from start to finish (he blended the top 2022 reds, but they weren’t his wines). A dinner was convened at the excellent Labombe by Trivet restaurant (newly Michelin starred) and we tasted through them.

Luke chose a tricky vintage for the first one entirely under his control: the 2023. This was the vintage where it rained and rained, and not at the right time. The weather in December was almost biblical, with thunderstorms and fires. It cooled off, and then in February it started raining. In the February-April harvest period of 2023 there were 171 mm of rain. By comparison, 2022 saw 51 mm, 2024 saw 59 mm and 2025 saw 55 mm. With picking delayed, Luke says it gave him a lot of time to walk the vineyards. ‘I learned a lot from it,’ he says, and was averaging 35 000 steps a day getting to know the property. And in the end, he managed to get some good grapes into the cellar. ‘2023 is lighter and more elegant in style,’ he says, ‘with a red fruit spectrum. ‘2024 has a lot more density.’

Vergelegen is technically in Stellenbosch, but it has a unique microclimate. Closer to the ocean, it is much cooler than the rest of Stellenbosch, and the climate is closer to Elgin than the Simonsberg.

Luke grew up in Constantia. ‘I thought I understood turbulent weather,’ he says, ‘until I came to this estate. It’s raw and turbulent.’

Vergelegen Chardonnay 2024 Stellenbosch, South Africa
This is bright and fruit-focused with lovely citrus fruit. It has a bright stoniness and good acidity. This is chiselled and fresh with nice finesse and purity. Great acid line here: it’s a very focused wine with lovely purity. 93/100

Vergelegen Estate White Blend 2024 Stellenbosch, South Africa
52% Semillon, 48% Sauvignon. This is lovely with amazing acidity (the pH is below 3), nice purity, a touch of smoky grapefruit and chiselled acidity. Lovely lemon and pear fruit with a subtle hint of wax. Really fine. 95/100

Vergelegen Reserve Merlot 2023 Stellenbosch, South Africa
Picked at the beginning of when the rains started. So bright and pure with red cherries, a touch of raspberry brightness, and then a subtle chalky edge to the palate. This has some structure, but it’s all about the beautiful, expressive red fruits. There’s a bit of chalkiness on the mid-palate that works really well. 93/100

Vergelegen Reserve Cabernet Sauvignon 2024 Stellenbosch, South Africa
This is Cabernet from Clovelly and Hutton soils. Lovely vibrant, aromatic nose with blackcurrant fruit and some leafy green notes. It’s really expressive with sweet fruit on the palate and lovely purity. Grainy structure is well covered by the fruit, and it has a core of sweetness. Grainy with some tannic structure on the finish. Really appealing. 93/100

Vergelegen Estate Red 2023 Stellenbosch, South Africa
41% Cab 39 M 10 CF 5Malbec 5 Petit Verdot. This has a really appealing gravelly, chalky edge to the sweet blackberry and blackcurrant fruit. It has fantastic drinkability, but also some nice structure. There’s a nice lightness and freshness from the vintage, but there’s also substantial sweet fruit, with a nice acid line and fine structure. 94/100

Vergelegen ‘V’ 2023 Stellenbosch, South Africa
Cabernet Sauvignon from Glenrosa and Pineneedle soils. 18 months in oak, 30% new. These give a darker expression of Cabernet, and also a bit of sappiness. Concentrated and dense with firm tannins sitting under a wall of sweet blackcurrant fruit. Grippy and substantial, but also with good structure. There’s a freshness here, and it’s quite expressive. Very serious, and with all this structure, lots of potential. 95/100

Vergelegen ‘V’ 2024 Stellenbosch, South Africa
2% Petit Verdot in the blend. Concentrated and intense with lovely brightness. Firm tannins and fresh acidity underneath concentrated blackcurrant fruit, with a sweetness to the fruit. There’s a similar sappiness here that complements the bold fruit. And while it’s tannic, the structure is cloaked by the sweet fruit. Grippy but refined. In time this may well eclipse the 2023 (I’m tasting this very young). 95/100

title: Torbreck: a deep dive into this important Barossa winery, with winemaker Ian Hongell
summary: It was back in 1994 that Dave Powell began a winery that was to prove very important for the Barossa
content:
It was back in 1994 that Dave Powell began a winery that was to prove very important for the Barossa Valley. He had entered the wine business by accident: after studying economics, he did a vintage at Yalumba in 1981. He followed this up by travelling, working many more vintages, before returning to the Barossa to work at Rockford in 1992.

Powell became aware of various old neglected vineyards in the Barossa, and he started buying them up and making wine from them. His first wines were sold to an English guy he’d worked with at Saltram, Phil Reedman. His 1994 Shiraz and 1995 Grenache went to Australian Wine Agencies, and in 1997 the first Torbreck wines were released, the 1995 Runrig and 1996 Steading.

Torbreck grew considerably, and by the time I visited for the first time in 2004, they were processing 1000 tons, which is a significant quantity for a premium winery.  

Some notes on the names. Torbreck is the name of the forest in Scotland where Powell worked as a woodcutter. Steading is the name of a pub in Edinburgh; also the term given to an outbuilding on a farm. Struie is a mountain he worked on in Scotland; Factor is the guy who runs the woodcutting operation. Descendant is descended from Runrig: it uses the old barrels from Runrig and the vines were cuttings from the Runrig vines. And Runrig? In one of the pubs Powell drunk in on Struie the band Runrig played, only for the pub to burn down shortly afterwards. It’s also the name of the land distribution system that evolved into the crofting system. 

Powell collected old vineyards and renovated them, and then in the winery made striking wines combining bold, ripe flavours with focus and freshness, not an easy task. Torbreck’s wines were often high in alcohol, but they avoided jamminess: the concentrated flavours provided that balance and structure that these sorts of wines need.

Powell sold the winery in 2003, then bought it back with funds from a US investor in 2008. In 2014, the relationship with the investor broke down and Powell ended up leaving with nothing. He’s since started new projects, but Torbreck has continued to produce acclaimed wines, under the stewardship of current chief winemaker Ian Hongell (pictured above), who presented this extensive tasting, including mini-verticals of some of the top wines.

Hongell emphasizes that one of the reasons Torbreck are performing at such a high level is because of the close integration of vineyard and winery teams. Chief viticulturist is Nigel Blieschke. The two worked together for 8 years at Peter Lehmann before Blieschke moved to Torbreck in 2015, to be followed by Hongell in 2016. ‘One of our great strengths is the connection between viticulture and winemaking,’ says Hongell.

Initially, he says the frustration was the big fluctuations in yields between seasons, with the low yielding years that followed higher cropping years producing the best wines. So the two decided to do something about this, to increase the consistency and resilience of the 133 hectares (across five sites) that they own. They also source about the same vineyard area from a network of 32 growers. Most of the grapes come from the western ridge of the Barossa, with 15% coming from the Eden Valley.

They began by implementing no-till management, growing a self-seeding cover crop of rye grass, clover, medics and legumes. They brought sheep in outside the growing season to control this, eliminating several tractor passes at the same time. They also developed vegetation areas around vineyards where they sowed native species to act as an insectarium, creating effective biodiversity to reduce the need for spraying.

Hongell says that they stopped using artificial fertlizers, and instead spread 12 000 m3 of green waste (forest floor mulch) which they apply under the vines, and also add vermicast, cow manure compost and kelp to provide nutrients.

To protect the old vines and ensure the future health of the vineyard they have employed Simonit and Sirch to train up pruners, and these five pruners do 180 000 vines a year. They have 25 hectares of centenarian vines, and gaps in these old vineyards are filled by layering. This is possible because there is no phylloxera in the Barossa. They have planted 25 hectares of Shiraz from cuttings taken from heritage vineyards, as well as 10 hectares of Grenache and 5 hectares of Mataro.

Harvest is quite late. Hongell is looking for flavour and phenolic stability, getting full physiological ripeness before the fruit collapses.

In the winery, red ferments are open-top, and are left to go wild for a few days before inoculating with cultured yeast. There’s an acid adjustment on the skins, then a further one after going to barrel, and then one before bottling. In all, typical adds of tartaric acid will be 1.5 g/litre.

The ferments are pumped over twice daily, and then when the wine is dry, it’s baset pressed, the press fraction is combined with the free run, and it goes to barrel. Malolactic fermentation is in barrel, and once this is done, the wine is racked, the lees are removed, and the wine goes back to barrel where it receives a sulfur dioxide add, the acidity is adjusted, and it is left without topping up. Hongell likes a bit of acetaldehyde development, and then this is dealt with by the pre-bottling sulfur dioxide add.

Torbreck The Steading Blanc 2025 Barossa, Australia
12.2% alcohol. 60% Roussanne, 37% Marsanne, 3% Viognier. Juicy and bright with nice pear and citrus fruit. Compact, crisp and alert with a lemony edge. 92/100

Torbreck Hillside Vineyard Grenache 2024 Barossa, Australia
15% alcohol. A high vineyard near Lyndoch with dark red clay with shale, quartzite and ironstone. Supple and juicy with notes of redcurrant and red berries. Real elegance here. Supple and well balanced. 94/100

Torbreck Les Amis 2023 Barossa, Australia
15.5% alcohol. Devigorated soils, western Greenock. Grenache. Nice weight and freshness with red cherry and redcurrant fruit, with a core of sweet fruit. Nice crunch here with a peppery edge to the herb-tinged fruit. 94/100

Torbreck The Steading 2024 Barossa, Australia
15.5% alcohol. Grenache (57%), Shiraz (33%) and Mataro (10%). Complex and layered with a core of sweet berry and cherry fruit, with some herbal hints and a touch of pepper. A classic Barossa red blend, done really well. 94/100

Torbreck The Pict 2023 Barossa, Australia
15.5% alcohol. 100% Mataro. Ripe and lush with nice depth, and a spicy bite. Warm, rich and bold, but shows Mataros herby, peppery, spicy side, too. Impactful. 94/100

Torbreck The Gask 2024 Barossa, Australia
15% alcohol. This is Shiraz from the Eden Valley. Bold and rich with spice, herbs, black cherries and plums. It’s peppery with nice depth of fruit but also a sense of freshness. Beautifully layered cherry and berry fruits. Nicely structured. 96/100

The Descendent is a single vineyard next to the winery in Marananga, planted in 1994 with cuttings taken from the Runrig vineyard. It has four bands of white grapes planted through it, including two rows of Viognier used for this wine: Torbreck were the first to co-ferment Shiraz and Viognier in the Barossa with Runrig, and this wine follows in these footsteps. The Viognier component is pressed and the wet skins are added to the Shiraz fermenter, and the percentage indicated here is by weight.

Torbreck The Descendent 2021 Barossa, Australia
15% alcohol. 94% Shiraz, 6% Viognier. From Marananga. 20 months in barrels previously used for Runrig. This is lush, ripe and rich with blackberry and black cherry fruit. Bold, rich and sleek with sweet ripe fruit showing some savoury olive and spice notes. Expansive, showing a hint of apricot richness, and then charcoal and smoke on the finish. 94/100

Torbreck The Descendent 2022 Barossa, Australia
15% alcohol. 92% Shiraz, 8% Viognier. This is bold and rich with lovely softness to the lush blackberry fruit. Salty and rich with some floral and apricot notes, as well as liquorice notes and bold black fruit. There’s freshness and florality here. So enticing. 96/100

Torbreck The Descendent 2023 Barossa, Australia
15% alcohol. 96% Shiraz, 4% Viognier. Amazing floral apricot, black cherry and blackberry fruit on the nose. So exotic. The palate has ripe black fruits but also great definition, with a slight gravelly, savoury edge. 96/100

The Factor is Shiraz from five sites, two of which Torbreck own, in Gomersal, Krondorf, Marananga and Greenock. Many of these vines were planted in the 19th century. Two years in barrels, 40% new (French oak).

Torbreck The Factor 2021 Barossa, Australia
15% alcohol. Rich, vivid and spicy with nice density. Shows black cherries and plums with some oak adding an extra level of flavour. Rich with a salty finish, and some warmth. 94/100

Torbreck The Factor 2022 Barossa, Australia
15.5% alcohol. Fresh, floral and aromatic with rich blackberry and cherry fruit. Grippy and structured on the palate but also showing lush fruit, with hints of liquorice. Textural and finely spiced. 95/100

Torbreck The Factor 2023 Barossa, Australia
15.5% alcohol. Lush and inviting, ripe and floral, with blackberry fruit and some saltiness. There’s a rich texture and density here with fresh fruit. 95/100

Runrig is Shiraz from old, dry-grown vineyards. The first release was from a single vineyard, but now it comes from six vineyards, three of which are owned by Torbreck. The vineyards were planted between 1858 and 1910. French oak maturation, 50% new.

Torbreck Runrig 2021 Barossa, Australia
98% Shiraz, 2% Viognier. Vivid, bold and rich with sweet black cherry and blackberry fruit, and some ashy, charcoal and spice detail. Concentrated with some red fruit character in the mix. Intense with fine tannins but plenty of them, finishing salty with some iodine, and a bit of peachy exoticism. 95/100

Torbreck Runrig 2022 Barossa, Australia
98% Shiraz, 2% Viognier. Fresh and floral with black and red cherries, showing some finesse and freshness on the nose. It’s ripe and concentrated but there’s a real finesse to the tannins and a salty, liquorice edge that adds interest. Som apricot and peach hints add an exotic quality to the fruit. 96/100

Torbreck Runrig 2023 Barossa, Australia
99% Shiraz, 1% Viognier. This is luscious and fine with black cherry and blackberry fruit, showing amazing texture, and a spicy framing to the fruit. It’s still quite primary with lovely fruit and a nice grainy structure. 96/100

Torbreck The Forebear 2021 Barossa, Australia
15% alcohol. This is from Torbreck’s oldest single vineyard, planted in Lyndoch in 1850. It was first made in 2019, and half is aged in new French oak, half in new Stockinger (Austrian oak). Aromatic, intense and floral with sweet black cherry and blackberry fruit. Linear and textural with some creaminess. Real concentration and intensity with nice salty detail. Sleek and polished, ripe and vivid, amazing focus. 97/100

Torbreck The Laird 2021 Barossa, Australia
15.5% alcohol. This is from the Gnadenfrei vineyard, planted with Shiraz in 1958. It spends three years in new barrels from Dominique Laurent, known as ‘magic casks’. This is vivid with ripe black cherry and blackberry fruit, some saltiness, and spicy, cedary woody notes, as well as a hint of clove. Vivid and spice laden, showing a lot of smoky oak. The oak is winning at the moment, but this may well resolve with time. 93/100

title: Video: Pannobile – meeting this coalition of amazing Burgenland producers, who are making some of Austria’s most interesting wines
summary: A group of international wine folk gathered at Claus Preisinger’s winery in Gols to meet the Pannobile group. This is
content: A group of international wine folk gathered at Claus Preisinger’s winery in Gols to meet the Pannobile group. This is a coalition of Burgenland producers making some of Austria’s most interesting wines, with Judith Beck, Rennasistas, Heinrich, Gsellman, Leitner, Nittnaus and Pittnauer as well as Priesinger.

title: Spotlight on Saint-Joseph: tasting 39 different wines from this northern Rhône cru
summary: It was back in 1956 that Saint-Joseph became an appellation, and the original classification was expanded in 1969 from the
content:
It was back in 1956 that Saint-Joseph became an appellation, and the original classification was expanded in 1969 from the initial six communes to the size that it is today.

It’s a long, thin string of vineyards situated on the right bank of the Rhône, stretching some 60 km from, Chavanay in the north to across the river from Valence in the south, including 26 villages.

The latest stats (2025) show that there are 1504 hectares of vines, of which 16% are now farmed organically and biodynamically.

Granite is the main story here: the hillside vineyards are the result of the Rhône river hitting the edge of the Massif Central, a huge slab of granite derived from ancient volcanic activity that sits across a lot of the middle of France. When the Rhône hits this, it has nowhere to go, so heads down south at a rapid rate.

The vineyards tend to be sloped, and there’s a succession of them along the 60 km of the appellation.

Reds are Syrah, and whites are made from either Marsanne or Roussanne, or blends of the two.

These are my notes on 39 wines tried at an event in London celebrating the 70th birthday of the appellation. The standard was consistently high, especially with the reds, although I’d like to see maybe a bit less oak used, and also some more whole bunch for freshness. Alternative elevage – large casks, concrete and clay – works very well for Syrah and helps keep the wines more focused, something that is likely to be needed with climate change.

Red wines

Maison Bruyère & David Les Parcelles 2022 Saint-Joseph, Northern Rhône, France
From Charnas and Chavanay, 100% destemmed, aged in 500 litre barrels, 20% new. Juicy, fresh and supple with some roast meat character and assertive cherry and berry fruits. Quite a classic style. 92/100

Cave de Tain Parcellaires Esprit de Granit 2022 Saint-Joseph, Northern Rhône, France
Plot selection of old vines, aged in 400 litre barrels, 33% new. There’s some oak in the mix here, with ripe, sweet black fruits, some pepper, and some grip. Stylish. 92/100

Aymeric Paillard La Vigne des Pères ‘Petit Père’ 2022 Saint-Joseph, Northern Rhône, France
From Sarras and the Châtaigner Lieu dit in Ozon. Whole bunch, semi-carbonic, then two years in barrel. Low intervention. Grippy, grainy and dark, with a savoury, spicy overlay to the fruit. Quite angular and meaty. 90/100

Yann Chave Saint-Joseph 2023 Northern Rhône, France
From a 2 hectare lieu dit called Les Pierres in Tournon. Echalas training, 30 year old vines. Destemmed. 18 months ageing in 600 litre barrels and wine globes. Spicy, meaty, peppery nose with some grip. Very stylish and expressive with a bit of grip here. 95/100

Yann Chave Saint-Joseph 2024 Northern Rhône, France
Supple and bright with nice peppery detail, and some grippy structure under the sweet, balanced black cherry and berry fruits. Savoury and slightly peppery, with nice focus and grippy structure. 94/100

Domaine Courbis Saint-Joseph ‘Les Royes’ 2023 Northern Rhône, France
A 7 hectare vineyard at the southern end of the appellation. Chalky clay soils, which are unusual here. Destemmed and then barrel aged (30% new). Supple and elegant, this is peppery with nice ripeness to the well balanced sweet berry fruits, with a touch of olive on the finish. 93/100

Domaine Colombet Saint-Joseph Réminiscence 2023 Northern Rhône, France
From Malleval and Saint Pierre de Boeuf. 5% new oak. Lovely intensity to this wine. Peppery and bright with appealing cherry fruit, good structure and some raspberry freshness. Detailed with nice energy. 95/100

Domaine J Denuzière Saint-Joseph 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Partial destemming, then ageing in tank and used oak. Juicy, supple and bright with sweet cherry and berry fruit as well as a hint of spiciness. Nice brightness: made in an elegant style. 93/100

Domaine Graeme & Julie Bott Saint-Joseph 2023 Northern Rhône, France
From Chavanay, St Pierre de Boeuf, Talencieux. 30% whole bunch, wild yeast, 15 months in small French oak. Bright and alert with vivid red fruits and some peppery detail. The palate is supple and elegant, with lovely poise. Real energy here. 94/100

Domaine Pierre Jean Villa Saint-Joseph Tildé 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Partially destemmed, aged 24 months in 225 and 500 litre oak. Open, meaty nose. Appealing, ripe palate with sweet cherry and berry fruits and some olive savourines. Ripe, nuanced and delicious. 94/100

Xavier Vignon Saint-Joseph 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Aged 15 months in barrel, 15% new. Ripe, sleek and inviting with appealing red cherry and strawberry fruit. Good ripeness and concentration. Very expressive in a ripe style. 93/100

Domaine Coursodon Saint-Joseph L’Olivaie 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Destemmed, aged 15 months in barrel, 15% new. Pepper, spice and black cherry with some olive intensity. There’s a slight meatiness, and also some woody hints in the background. 93/100

Domaine Coursodon Saint-Joseph La Sansonne 2023 Northern Rhône, France
100% new oak. Juicy and ripe with enticing sweet berries and cherries. Very ripe and luscious, made in an appealing, forward style. 93/100

Pierre Gaillard Saint-Joseph Les Pierres 2023 Northern Rhône, France
From Malleval and Chavanay, 18 months in 100% new oak. Meaty, intense and detailed with pepper and spice notes. Vibrant with good concentration and a pepper-laced finish. Rich but also complex, and it swallows the new oak very well. 95/100

Pierre Gaillard Saint-Joseph Clos de Cuminalle 2023 Northern Rhône, France
From a 3 hectare vineyard in Chavanay facing east, planted in 1981. Destemmed. 30% new oak. Loveluy focus and intensity here: floral, expressive and peppery with some olive tapenade notes and also some good structure. Very stylish. 95/100

Cave Saint-Désirat Saint-Joseph 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Destemmed, aged in stainless steel tanks. Juicy and bright with vivid cherry and berry fruits, backed by nice tannins and good acidity. There’s some brightness here. 92/100

M Chapoutier Saint-Joseph Les Granilites 2023 Northern Rhône, France
A blend of parcels mainly in the south of the appellation. Small and large barrels (20%) new and 20% in unlined concrete. Fresh, brooding black cherry and pepper, with some crushed rock minerality. Vibrant and complex, with lovely peppery detail. 95/100

Maison André Perret Saint-Joseph Les Grisières 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Plump, ripe and delicious with a core of sweet cherry and berry fruit, and a slight smoky edge to the ripe, plush fruit. 93/100

Domaine Jean-Claude Marsanne Saint-Joseph 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Destemmed, aged in small and larger oak. Lovely elegant style with sweet cherry and plum fruit, and a twist of pepper. Sleek and ripe, and well balanced. 93/100

Domaine Luyton-Fleury Saint-Joseph Origine 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Plot selection between Mauves and Tournon. Very old vines, aged 20 months in barrel, 20% new. Bright and fresh with some meaty, peppery detail and cherry and raspberry fruit. Very appealing. 93/100

Domaine Luyton-Fleury Saint-Joseph Les Cerisiers 2024 Northern Rhône, France
Attractive bold cherry and plum fruit with some peppery detail. Sleek and refined with spicy notes, and a peppery finish. 93/100

Domaine Pierre Jean Villa Saint-Joseph Préface 2024 Northern Rhône, France
Sweetly fruited with some oaky notes. Supple and ripe, and quite polished with a grainy, well defined finish. 92/100

Domaine des Remezières Saint-Joseph 2024 Northern Rhône, France
30% new oak. Ripe, fresh style with sleek cherry and berry fruit, as well as some peppery hints. Lovely texture and focus with sleek black fruits. 94/100

Domaine du Chêne Saint-Joseph Anaïs 2024 Northern Rhône, France
Aged 18 months in new oak. Vivid, fresh and floral with nice raspberry and cherry fruit, as well as a hint of strawberry richness. A ripe style but nicely fleshy and floral. 93/100

Vignoble Deleu Saint-Joseph Exorde 2024 Northern Rhône, France
From Serrières and Malleval. Forward, with bright cherry and raspberry fruit, with some spicy oak and a firm finish. 91/100

Domaine François Grenier Saint-Joseph Grange Bara 2024 Northern Rhône, France
One-third new oak. A ripe style with some oak impact. Spicy and rich, and quite brooding with a peppery finish. 92/100

Domaine Monier-Perreol Saint-Joseph Tradition 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Vivid and spicy with some meatiness. Brooding with a savoury edge to the black fruits. Peppery and grippy, this is ever so slightly rustic, in a good way. 94/100

Domaine du Tunel Saint-Joseph 2024 Northern Rhône, France
Tournel, Mauves and Glun. Vivid black cherry fruit with some peppery character. Ripe but focused with real elegance to the ripe black fruits. Serious. 95/100

Domaine Jolivet Saint-Joseph L’instinct 2024 Northern Rhône, France
90% granite soils, 10% loess. 5% new oak. Nice juicy edge to the sweet, well balanced black cherry and plum fruit, with nice grainy structure. Has balance. 93/100br

White wines

Domaine J Denuzière Saint-Joseph Blanc 2022 Northern Rhône, France
100% Marsanne, 25% barrel fermented, 75% stainless steel. Classic style with some oak supporting the dense pear and peach fruit. Nice weight. 92/100

M Chapoutier Saint-Joseph Blanc Les Granilites 2022 Northern Rhône, France
100% Marsanne. 80% barrel fermented (10% new), 20% stainless steel. Showing some development with appley, oxidative hints as well as stony, mineral laced pear and peach fruit. 90/100

Cave de Tain Saint-Joseph Blanc Parcellaires Terre d’Ivoire 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Granite terraces with some limestone. Fermented in used 400 litre oak. 91% Rousanne, 9% Marsanne. Nice weight with some oaky notes in the background as well as pear and peach fruit. Textural and engrossing. 93/100

Paul Jaboulet Ainé Saint-Joseph Blanc Le Grand Pompée 2024 Northern Rhône, France
Barrels, stainless steel and concrete eggs. A fresh, textured wine with sweet pear and citrus fruit. Nice mouthfeel with some weight in the mouth. 92/100

Domaine Colombet Saint-Joseph Blanc Promesse 2024 Northern Rhône, France
75% Roussanne, 25% Marsanne, single plot on top of Saint Pierre de Boeuf. Roussanne aged in oak, Marsanne in stainless steel. Intriguing fennel notes combine with ripe pears. Exotic fruity palate with lovely ripeness and detail. Real personality here. 94/100

Domaine François Grenier Saint-Joseph Blanc Fragrance 2024 Northern Rhône, France
80% Roussanne, 20% Marsanne. Nice texture here with yellow plum fruit and some pear, with a mineral twist on the finish. 92/100

Domaine Courbis Saint-Joseph Blanc Les Royes 2024 Northern Rhône, France
Chalky clay soil. Marsanne, 50% new oak. Supple, showing nice weight, and some coconut and spice from the oak. Fine grainy structure. 92/100

Domaine Graeme & Julie Bott Saint-Joseph Blanc 2024 Northern Rhône, France
70% Roussanne, 30% Marsanne. Chavanay and Saint Pierre de Boeuf. Textured, fine grained, showing appealing ripe pear and yellow plum fruit. Stylish with nice focus. 94/100

Hugo and Pauline Vila Saint-Joseph Blanc Marsanne 2024 Northern Rhône, France
Textured and rich with pear and white peach fruit, showing good density in the mouth. Has a nice spicy overlay with well integrated oak notes. 92/100

Domaine Jean-Claude Marsanne Saint-Joseph Blanc 2023 Northern Rhône, France
Fresh, stony and bright with nice green apple and pear fruit. Juicy and linear. 92/100

title: Matthias Müller, stunning wines from the Mittelrhein in Germany
summary: Website: https://www.weingut-matthiasmueller.de/en/start-en/ Matthias Müller is a family-owned wine estate based in the Mittelrhein. It’s a beautiful area, and this stretch
content:
Website: https://www.weingut-matthiasmueller.de/en/start-en/

Matthias Müller is a family-owned wine estate based in the Mittelrhein.

It’s a beautiful area, and this stretch of vineyards on the Rhine between Bingen and Koblenz was awarded UNESCO World Heritage status in 2002.

They are based in Spy, and have 20 hectares of vines in the Bopparder Hamm, all on steeply sloping, south-facing slate soils. This includes sites in the top single vineyards of Engelstein, Rabenlei, Ohlenberg, Feuerlay and Mandelstein.

They also have a separate brand, Sektmanufaktur Momentum, for sparkling.

I tasted with Johannes Müller at the Cool Climate symposium in Copenhagen.  

Momentum Riesling Brut 2023 Mittelrhein, Germany
Lively, bright and focused with nice lemon and grapefruit notes. Juicy and expressive with nice purity. 91/100

Momentum Blanc de Blanc Brut Nature 2020 Mittelrhein, Germany
Pinot Blanc and Chardonnay, half aged in old oak, 4.5 years on lees. This is bright and linear with a slight pithiness. Dry, juicy and mineral with nice presence. 92/100

Matthias Müller Rhein Schiefer Riesling Trocken 2024 Mittelrhein, Germany
11% alcohol. Slate soils. Textured with nice mineral notes and a rounded body. Lovely presence here: easy but serious at the same time. 92/100

Matthias Müller Bopparder Ham Riesling Trocken Steilstück 2024 Mittelrhein, Germany
Aged in stainless steel. This has texture and balance. Ripe but with good tension, showing textured crystalline citrus fruit. 93/100

Matthias Müller Bopparder Ham Fässerlay Riesling 2024 Mittelrhein, Germany
East-facing hill. Very textural, fine and crystalline with a saline twist. Liquid minerals, with a lovely lemony focus. Very fine. 94/100

Matthias Müller Bopparder Hamm Mandelstein GG Riesling Trocken 2024 Mittelrhein, Germany
Linear, stony and mineral. Refined and crystalline with precision and detail. Has ripeness but also finesse, and some salinity. 95/100

Matthias Müller Bopparder Hamm Feuerlay GG Riesling Trocken 2023 Mittelrhein, Germany
This is focused, linear and bright but also has a touch of apricot richness, and some creamy texture. Lively and crystalline with great precision. Salty and mineral, showing nice intensity and finesse. Very fine. 95/100

title: Gamine Rose, innovative wines from the Dão and Douro
summary: Odile Rose Bouchard is the winegrower behind Gamine Rose. Her family own Quinta do Tedo, a small Port estate in
content:
Odile Rose Bouchard is the winegrower behind Gamine Rose. Her family own Quinta do Tedo, a small Port estate in the Douro Valley, and she began making her own wines in 2021, in both the Dão region, and also with grapes from the family property.

Odile has a varied backround, having lived in California, Tuscany and Burgundy as well as the Douro, and this is reflected in her thoughtful, creative winemaking. I tasted with her at Simplesmente Vinho in 2026, where she was just showing her Douro wines.

Gamine Rose Fora do Tedo Branco 2024 Douro, Portugal
This wine spends 10 months on lees in barrel, wine globe and amphora. Lively, pure and vivid with juicy pear and lemon. Lovely personality and poise here. Very fine. 94/100

Gamine Rose Tedo Vinho Tinto Batard 2024 Douro, Portugal
This Bastardo plot was planted 2022 at Quinta do Tedo, so this is the first crop. Fresh and vivid with nice textural berry fruits with some grainy structure. Really impressive. 93/100

Gamine Rose CP 2024 Douro, Portugal
This was raised in concrete amphora, made by Drunk Turtle. It’s Touriga Franca at 350 m, with transition schist and granite soils. Floral and intense with nice vibrant cherry fruit and some texture, showing a core of sweet fruit. Fresh and beautifully balanced. 94/100

Gamine Rose Q+V 2024 Douro, Portugal
This is from the same plot of Touriga Franca, this time with some whole cluster and aged in 600 litre barrels then glass. Structural, fresh, linear and with beautiful cherry fruit. Such a lovely wine. 94/100

title: Silice, beautiful natural wines from Ribeira Sacra, Spain
summary: Silice is the project of Fredi Torres (pictured above), and is based in Ribeira Sacra. He began Silice in 2013
content:
Silice is the project of Fredi Torres (pictured above), and is based in Ribeira Sacra. He began Silice in 2013 after some beers in a bar, and it is a collaboration between him and two land-owning brothers. Grapes are also sourced from other local growers. Fredi is looking for a fresh style, and all the ferments are with natural yeasts.

Silice Blanco 2024
Treixadura and Albariño plus others. Raised in concrete. Lively, pure and transparent with fresh lemon and some green apple. Really elegant style. 93/100

Silice Tinto 2023
Mencia with 15% other varieties in the blend. Lovely floral cherry aromatics with some dried herb. Elegant juicy style with a silky edge to the cherry fruit. Nice balance. 93/100

Silice Xabrega 2022
Red granite with mica soils. 100% whole bunch. Pale red in colour. So elegant and refined, with juicy red cherries, fine herbs and some mineral framing. 94/100

Silice Lobeiras 2019
From a very steep vineyard. 100% whole bunch. Supple, fine and lightly boned with lovely elegant red cherry fruit, as well as some redcurrant. So fine, with some savouriness emerging. 94/100

Silice Rosende 2019
Garnacha Tintorea (aka Alicante Bouschet). Nice intensity with a savoury spicy edge to the vivid, tart berry fruits. Grippy. 93/100
